entry.title: Former Saydel School IT Employee Sentenced for Post-Employment Hacks
entry.content: This digest window centers on an insider-threat case involving a former school district IT employee who retained access after leaving the organization and used it to disrupt systems over an extended period. The reporting highlights the operational impact of lingering administrative access in education environments, including disruption to identity, device management, learning platforms, and public-facing accounts. A former senior IT support specialist for Saydel Community School District, Ezekiel Dean Potter, was sentenced to 21 months in prison for cyberattacks against his former employer. Prosecutors said Potter retained credentials after his employment ended and targeted district systems over a 21-month period, causing disruptions across administrative, instructional, and communications platforms. According to court documents, Potter deleted the district’s Facebook page and disrupted access to educational platforms and accounts. He also deleted data from the district’s Apple School Manager account, which prevented staff from accessing the platform and disabled management of district MacBooks and iPads for roughly a week. The case also involved compromise of Google administrator accounts used to access the district’s Schoology learning management system. In January 2025, Potter used that access to delete employee Gmail accounts, disrupting teacher access and broader district operations. Potter was ordered to serve three years of supervised release following his prison term and to pay $59,668.81 in restitution to the Saydel Community School District and its insurer. The restitution reflects remediation costs associated with restoring affected services and addressing the damage caused by the unauthorized activity.
entry.summary: This digest window centers on an insider-threat case involving a former school district IT employee who retained access after leaving the organization and used it to disrupt systems over an extended period. The reporting highlights the operational impact of lingering administrative access in...

entry.title: Velvet Ant Operation Highland Backdoored Critical Infrastructure Authentication for a Decade
entry.content: This digest centers on a long-running Chinese-linked espionage operation targeting an isolated critical infrastructure network. Reporting from Sygnia describes how Velvet Ant sustained access for approximately 10 years by compromising authentication paths, modifying trusted Linux components, and creating covert remote execution routes into a segregated environment. The case highlights a mature persistence model focused less on noisy malware deployment and more on deep integration with identity, access, and network control planes. For technical defenders and executives alike, the incident illustrates how isolation alone can be undermined when authentication flows and administrative infrastructure are subverted. Sygnia attributed the campaign, dubbed Operation Highland, to the Chinese-linked Velvet Ant threat group. The activity reportedly affected a large organization’s isolated critical infrastructure network, where the attackers maintained espionage access for a decade. The operation began with compromise of internet-facing systems before the attackers pivoted inward using a modified GS-Netcat reverse shell and a custom SOCKS5 proxy. The campaign’s most significant feature was the abuse of authentication and application infrastructure to reach systems that were not directly exposed. Velvet Ant modified Nginx and FastCGI configurations to create a remote-execution pathway into the segregated environment through crafted HTTP requests. This allowed interaction with the isolated network without requiring a direct connection to the critical infrastructure segment. Sygnia also reported that the attackers replaced legitimate Linux PAM modules and OpenSSH components with trojanized versions. These backdoored components accepted hardcoded passwords, harvested credentials, captured commands, and preserved persistent access. By embedding themselves into core authentication mechanisms, the operators gained durable control over administrative pathways while blending into expected system behavior. The operation demonstrates a persistence strategy built around the compromise of trusted binaries and configuration layers rather than reliance on standalone implants alone. Backdoored PAM and OpenSSH components gave Velvet Ant access at the point where users and administrators authenticated, creating opportunities to collect credentials and observe operator activity. The use of modified web and FastCGI configurations further expanded the attackers’ ability to execute commands through infrastructure that would normally be considered part of legitimate service delivery. Remediation was described as complex because the compromised components were central to system authentication and remote administration. Sygnia noted that replacing many trojanized critical components could risk breaking authentication, locking out legitimate administrators, or causing operational outages. In the context of critical infrastructure, that operational fragility made eradication more difficult than simply removing a conventional malware payload. Overall, Operation Highland reflects an espionage pattern in which adversaries target the connective tissue between exposed enterprise systems and protected operational environments. The reported decade-long access underscores the strategic value of authentication infrastructure, reverse proxies, and administrative services as targets for stealthy persistence inside high-value networks.
entry.summary: This digest centers on a long-running Chinese-linked espionage operation targeting an isolated critical infrastructure network. Reporting from Sygnia describes how Velvet Ant sustained access for approximately 10 years by compromising authentication paths, modifying trusted Linux components, and...

entry.title: Splunk CVE-2026-20253, Salesforce Phishing Abuse, and Anthropic Fable 5 Suspension
entry.content: This coverage window is dominated by three distinct but operationally relevant developments: a critical unauthenticated Splunk Enterprise vulnerability, a phishing campaign abusing trusted Salesforce sending infrastructure, and a U.S. export-control action affecting access to Anthropic AI models. Together, the reports highlight continued exposure in enterprise telemetry platforms, the limits of email authentication when legitimate SaaS infrastructure is abused, and growing regulatory pressure around advanced AI model access. Splunk released security updates for CVE-2026-20253, a critical Splunk Enterprise flaw rated CVSS 9.8. The vulnerability affects Splunk Enterprise versions below 10.2.4 and 10.0.7, while Splunk Enterprise 10.4 is not affected. Splunk Cloud is also not impacted because the affected PostgreSQL sidecar architecture is not used in that product. The flaw allows an unauthenticated user to create or truncate arbitrary files through a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint. watchTowr Labs reported that the issue can be exploited through the /v1/postgres/recovery/backup and /v1/postgres/recovery/restore endpoints and can lead to pre-authenticated remote code execution on susceptible systems. Splunk Enterprise 10.0.0 through 10.0.6 are fixed in 10.0.7, and versions 10.2.0 through 10.2.3 are fixed in 10.2.4. The significance of the issue is amplified by Splunk’s common role in security monitoring, log aggregation, and incident response workflows. A pre-authentication code execution path in such a platform represents a high-value exposure because compromised monitoring infrastructure can affect both visibility and trust in downstream investigative data. IRONSCALES reported a credential-harvesting campaign that used a compromised or malicious Salesforce CRM organization to send an email disguised as a Medicare Transaction Facilitator attestation request. The message passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC with compauth=pass because Salesforce was an authorized sender for the outcomes.com domain. The email also used identical From and To addresses, aryan@outcomes.com, creating a self-send pattern through Salesforce. The campaign’s call-to-action linked to nexaroco.com/providerrelations/, which served an Office 365 credential-harvesting page. Themis initially flagged the message at 50% confidence, and additional phishing signals from the IRONSCALES community confirmed the classification. The activity illustrates how attackers can leverage trusted CRM infrastructure and valid domain authentication to reduce the effectiveness of controls that rely heavily on sender legitimacy. The reported lure also reflects a broader trend in phishing operations: attackers are increasingly aligning messages with business workflows that recipients may view as routine or compliance-driven. In this case, a healthcare- and Medicare-themed attestation request was paired with authenticated Salesforce delivery, increasing the appearance of legitimacy despite the credential theft destination. Anthropic suspended worldwide access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 AI models after receiving a U.S. government export-control directive ordering it to block access by foreign nationals. According to Anthropic, the directive applies to foreign nationals both inside and outside the United States, including the company’s own foreign-national employees. The company said this forced it to disable the models for all customers. Fable 5 had started rolling out on June 9 as a free limited-time offering for Pro, Max, and Enterprise customers through June 22 before being taken offline. Anthropic said new sessions would fall back to a user’s default model or Claude Opus 4.8, existing Fable 5 sessions would terminate with an error, and Platform requests to Fable 5 would fail. Anthropic disputed the rationale for the directive, stating that it appeared to stem from a narrow reported jailbreak involving a prompt to read a specific codebase and fix software flaws. The incident is notable for security teams tracking AI governance, model availability risk, and the intersection of software vulnerability analysis capabilities with export-control policy.
entry.summary: This coverage window is dominated by three distinct but operationally relevant developments: a critical unauthenticated Splunk Enterprise vulnerability, a phishing campaign abusing trusted Salesforce sending infrastructure, and a U.S. export-control action affecting access to Anthropic AI models....

entry.title: Atomic Arch AUR Hijack and Anthropic Claude Fable 5 Security Routing
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by two distinct but relevant security themes: software supply-chain compromise in developer ecosystems and the evolving role of frontier AI models in cybersecurity workflows. The most operationally significant item is the reported “Atomic Arch” campaign affecting more than 400 Arch User Repository packages, while the AI-focused reporting centers on Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 public release and continued restricted access to Claude Mythos 5. Security researchers and the Arch Linux community disclosed a June 2026 supply-chain attack involving more than 400 hijacked Arch User Repository packages. According to the reporting, attackers adopted orphaned AUR packages that already had users, then modified PKGBUILDs or install hooks to execute malicious npm dependencies, including packages named atomic-lockfile and js-digest. The malicious npm package reportedly contained a Rust-based credential-stealing payload. Targeted data included browser cookies, SSH keys, developer tokens, chat sessions, Docker or Podman credentials, and cloud access keys, making the campaign particularly relevant to developer workstations, self-hosted CI runners, Arch-based environments, and WSL2 Arch installations. The incident illustrates continued attacker interest in community-maintained package ecosystems where trust, maintainer turnover, and transitive execution paths can create opportunities for broad compromise. The campaign also reflects a common supply-chain pattern: attackers do not need to compromise a flagship upstream project when they can inherit abandoned packages with existing install bases and introduce malicious build-time or install-time behavior. Aikido’s analysis of Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 release focuses on the distinction between the publicly released Fable model and the more restricted Mythos-class access program. The article states that Anthropic released Claude Fable 5 publicly, but did not make Mythos Preview broadly available; instead, Claude Mythos 5 remains limited to the original Glasswing initiative companies and an additional cohort of roughly 150 organizations. The reporting also notes that Claude Fable 5 routes cybersecurity prompts and discussions to an Opus 4.8 model, which the author argues makes direct benchmarking of Fable 5 for cybersecurity capability misleading. By contrast, the article describes Mythos 5 as having reduced safeguards compared with Fable 5 while remaining restricted to selected organizations. The broader argument is that AI use in security is likely to concentrate less on standalone vulnerability discovery and more on operational security hygiene. The author frames vulnerability discovery as only a portion of the work needed to reduce exposure, with misconfigurations, exposed services, stale permissions, ghost assets, broken identity edges, and improvised SaaS workflows making up a larger share of defensive workload. The piece therefore positions Mythos and Fable 5 token consumption as more likely to support defensive scans, remediation workflows, exposure management, and security hygiene than attacker-driven scanning or exploitation.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by two distinct but relevant security themes: software supply-chain compromise in developer ecosystems and the evolving role of frontier AI models in cybersecurity workflows. The most operationally significant item is the reported “Atomic Arch” campaign affecting...

entry.title: CVE-2026-20253, Velvet Ant, Atomic Arch, and Anthropic Fable 5 Suspension
entry.content:
This 24-hour digest is dominated by four concrete security developments: a critical Splunk Enterprise pre-authentication flaw, a decade-long Chinese-linked espionage operation in an isolated critical infrastructure network, a large Arch User Repository supply-chain compromise, and U.S. government action forcing Anthropic to suspend access to Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5. The reporting also highlights persistent risks from trusted identity paths, including former employee access abuse and phishing sent through an authenticated Salesforce tenant. Splunk released fixes for CVE-2026-20253, a critical Splunk Enterprise vulnerability rated CVSS 9.8. The issue affects Splunk Enterprise 10.0.0 through 10.0.6 and 10.2.0 through 10.2.3, with fixes available in 10.0.7 and 10.2.4; Splunk Cloud is reported as unaffected because it does not use the relevant PostgreSQL sidecars. Splunk described the flaw as allowing an unauthenticated user to create or truncate arbitrary files through an unauthenticated PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint. watchTowr Labs reported that the /v1/postgres/recovery/backup and /v1/postgres/recovery/restore endpoints can be abused for pre-authenticated remote code execution, making the bug especially relevant for organizations running exposed or reachable Splunk Enterprise management components. Sygnia reported that the Chinese-linked Velvet Ant group maintained access to an isolated critical infrastructure network for 10 years in a campaign tracked as Operation Highland. The attackers initially compromised internet-facing systems, deployed a modified GS-Netcat reverse shell, and used a custom SOCKS5 proxy before modifying Nginx and FastCGI configurations to create an HTTP-based remote execution path into the segregated environment. The campaign’s persistence centered on trusted authentication components. Velvet Ant allegedly replaced legitimate Linux PAM modules and OpenSSH components with backdoored versions that accepted hardcoded passwords, harvested credentials, captured commands, and preserved access; Sygnia noted that remediation was operationally complex because replacing trojanized authentication components risked outages and administrator lockout. A separate insider-threat case underscored similar trust-boundary risks. Former Saydel Community School District IT employee Ezekiel Dean Potter was sentenced to 21 months in prison after prosecutors said he retained credentials and disrupted the district over 21 months, including deleting accounts and pages, interfering with Apple School Manager, Schoology, and Gmail access, and causing tens of thousands of dollars in remediation costs. StepSecurity reported a June 2026 supply-chain attack against the Arch User Repository, dubbed “Atomic Arch,” in which attackers hijacked more than 400 AUR packages. The attackers adopted orphaned packages with existing users and modified PKGBUILDs or install hooks to execute malicious npm dependencies such as atomic-lockfile and js-digest. The malicious npm package reportedly contained a Rust credential-stealing payload targeting browser cookies, SSH keys, developer tokens, chat sessions, Docker or Podman credentials, and cloud access keys. The reporting specifically called out Arch and Arch-based environments, including self-hosted CI runners and WSL2 Arch systems, as potentially relevant where AUR packages were installed during the affected period. IRONSCALES described a credential-harvesting campaign that abused Salesforce sending infrastructure and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC for the outcomes.com domain. The Medicare Transaction Facilitator attestation-themed message used a self-send pattern through Salesforce and linked to nexaroco.com/providerrelations/, which served an Office 365 credential harvesting page. Multiple reports covered Anthropic’s suspension of Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 access after receiving a U.S. government export-control directive requiring the company to block access by foreign nationals. Anthropic said the directive applied to foreign nationals inside and outside the United States, including its own foreign-national employees, leading the company to disable the models globally rather than selectively enforce access restrictions. Anthropic stated that the government concern appeared tied to a reported jailbreak involving asking Fable 5 to read a specific codebase and fix software flaws, but the company disputed that this represented a universal jailbreak or uniquely dangerous capability. Reporting from Socket noted the directive arrived on June 12 at 5:21 p.m. ET and that some users lost access mid-project or shortly after upgrading paid plans. Aikido’s analysis added context around the release, noting that Fable 5 was publicly released while Mythos Preview remained limited to Glasswing initiative participants and a second cohort of about 150 organizations. The article also argued that Fable 5 routes cybersecurity prompts to Opus 4.8, complicating cybersecurity benchmarking, and framed enterprise AI security usage as heavily centered on defensive workflows such as exposure management, remediation, and security hygiene.

Critical Splunk Enterprise Flaw Lets Attackers Run Code Without Authentication — Must Read

Splunk patched a critical Splunk Enterprise flaw, CVE-2026-20253, that could let unauthenticated attackers perform arbitrary file operations and potentially achieve remote code execution through exposed PostgreSQL sidecar endpoints. Affected users should upgrade to Splunk Enterprise 10.0.7 or 10.2.4 immediately; Splunk Cloud is not affected.

  • Splunk released security updates for CVE-2026-20253, a critical Splunk Enterprise vulnerability rated 9.8 on the CVSS scoring system.
  • The flaw affects Splunk Enterprise 10.0.0 through 10.0.6 and 10.2.0 through 10.2.3, and it is fixed in versions 10.0.7 and 10.2.4.
  • Splunk said the vulnerability allows an unauthenticated user to create or truncate arbitrary files through a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint that lacks authentication controls.
  • watchTowr Labs reported that the flaw can be exploited for pre-authenticated remote code execution through the /v1/postgres/recovery/backup and /v1/postgres/recovery/restore endpoints.
  • Splunk said Splunk Cloud is not impacted because Postgres sidecars are not used in the product.

Chinese hackers hijack auth flow, spy on isolated network for a decade — Must Read

Chinese-linked Velvet Ant hackers maintained access to an isolated critical infrastructure network for a decade by hijacking authentication components like PAM and OpenSSH, letting them steal credentials, bypass logins, and monitor admin activity. The case shows that compromised authentication systems can make containment and cleanup far harder than removing a typical foothold.

  • Sygnia researchers said the Chinese-linked Velvet Ant threat group maintained espionage access to an isolated critical infrastructure network of a large organization for 10 years in a campaign dubbed Operation Highland.
  • Velvet Ant initially compromised internet-facing systems, deployed a modified GS-Netcat reverse shell, and used a custom SOCKS5 proxy to pivot into internal systems.
  • The attackers modified Nginx and FastCGI configurations to create a remote-execution path into the segregated environment through HTTP requests without requiring a direct connection to the critical infrastructure network.
  • Velvet Ant replaced legitimate Linux PAM modules and OpenSSH components with backdoored versions that accepted hardcoded passwords, harvested credentials, captured commands, and enabled persistent access.
  • Sygnia said remediation was complicated because replacing many trojanized critical components risked breaking authentication, locking out legitimate administrators, and causing operational outages.

400+ AUR Packages Hijacked: What the “Atomic Arch” Campaign Means for Supply-Chain Security — Must Read

Attackers hijacked 400+ trusted AUR packages by adopting abandoned projects and adding malicious dependencies that steal developer and CI credentials. Any affected Arch-based host should be treated as compromised, rebuilt from clean media, and have exposed secrets rotated.

  • Security researchers and the Arch Linux community disclosed a June 2026 supply-chain attack in which attackers hijacked more than 400 Arch User Repository packages.
  • The attackers adopted orphaned AUR packages with existing users and modified PKGBUILDs or install hooks to run malicious npm dependencies such as atomic-lockfile and js-digest.
  • The malicious npm package contained a Rust credential-stealing payload that targeted browser cookies, SSH keys, developer tokens, chat sessions, Docker or Podman credentials, and cloud access keys.
  • The article says affected Arch or Arch-based systems, including self-hosted CI runners and WSL2 Arch environments, should be reviewed if they installed AUR packages during the relevant period.
  • The article recommends treating hosts where the payload may have run as root as compromised, rebuilding them from trusted media, and rotating exposed credentials.

US Gov asks Anthropic to ban 'foreign national' access to Fable, Mythos — Should Read

Anthropic suspended global access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models after a U.S. government export-control directive ordered it to block all foreign nationals, which the company says effectively makes the models unavailable to everyone. Anthropic disputes the rationale, saying the cited jailbreak evidence is narrow and insufficient, and is working to restore access.

  • Anthropic suspended access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 AI models worldwide after receiving a U.S. government export control directive ordering it to block access by foreign nationals.
  • Anthropic said the directive bars access to the models by foreign nationals inside or outside the United States, including the company’s own foreign-national employees, forcing it to disable the models for all customers.
  • Fable 5 had begun rolling out on June 9 as a free limited-time offering for Pro, Max, and Enterprise customers through June 22 before being taken offline.
  • Anthropic said new sessions would fall back to a user’s default model or Claude Opus 4.8, existing Fable 5 sessions would end with an error, and Platform requests to Fable 5 would fail.
  • Anthropic disputed the government’s rationale, saying the order appears to stem from a narrow reported jailbreak involving asking the model to read a specific codebase and fix software flaws.

A Medicare Attestation Request Sent Through Salesforce, Authenticated by the Victim's Own Domain — Should Read

Attackers used a compromised or malicious Salesforce organization authorized for the victim’s domain to send a fully authenticated Medicare-themed phishing email that bypassed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC. The link led to an Office 365 credential-harvesting page, showing that trusted SaaS sending infrastructure can make phishing look legitimate.

  • Attackers used a compromised or malicious Salesforce CRM organization to send a Medicare Transaction Facilitator attestation-themed credential harvesting email.
  • The email passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC with compauth=pass because Salesforce was an authorized sender for the outcomes.com domain.
  • The From and To addresses were both aryan@outcomes.com, indicating a self-send pattern through Salesforce.
  • The call-to-action link went to nexaroco.com/providerrelations/, which served an Office 365 credential harvesting page.
  • IRONSCALES Themis flagged the message at 50% confidence, and the IRONSCALES community added additional phishing signal.

Ex-school district employee jailed for hacks on former employer — May Read

A former IT employee of Iowa’s Saydel Community School District was sentenced to 21 months in prison for using retained credentials to disrupt school systems for nearly two years. He must also pay nearly $60,000 in restitution after deleting accounts and impairing access to platforms like Apple School Manager, Schoology, and Gmail.

  • Former Saydel Community School District IT employee Ezekiel Dean Potter was sentenced to 21 months in prison for conducting cyberattacks against the district after his employment ended.
  • Prosecutors said Potter retained access credentials and targeted the district’s systems over 21 months, disrupting classroom operations and causing tens of thousands of dollars in remediation costs.
  • The attacks included deleting the district’s Facebook page, disrupting Apple School Manager access, deleting Schoology and Gmail accounts, and attempting unauthorized access to other online services.
  • Investigators traced some activity to IP addresses associated with Potter’s other employers and found spreadsheets containing Saydel account usernames and passwords on a USB drive turned over by a former coworker.
  • Potter was ordered to serve three years of supervised release and pay $59,668.81 in restitution to the Saydel Community School District and its insurer.

Full Fathom Five: The context of Anthropic’s Mythos-class public release — Optional

Anthropic’s public Claude Fable 5 release is not the same as the restricted Mythos model, especially because cybersecurity prompts are routed to a safer model. The bigger takeaway is that AI’s near-term security impact is likely to be defensive exposure management and cleanup, not a massive zero-day hacking apocalypse.

  • Anthropic released Claude Fable 5 publicly, but it did not release Mythos Preview as a publicly available model.
  • Claude Mythos 5 remains limited to the original Glasswing initiative companies and a second cohort of about 150 additional organizations.
  • Claude Fable 5 routes cybersecurity prompts and discussions to an Opus 4.8 model, which the article says makes benchmarking Fable 5 for cybersecurity capabilities pointless.
  • The article argues that vulnerability discovery is only a minority of the security problems organizations need to solve, with many issues involving misconfigurations, exposed services, stale permissions, ghost assets, broken identity edges, and SaaS workflow problems.
  • The article argues that AI tokens are more likely to be spent on defensive scans, remediation workflows, exposure management, and security hygiene than on large-scale threat actor scans and attacks.
entry.summary: This 24-hour digest is dominated by four concrete security developments: a critical Splunk Enterprise pre-authentication flaw, a decade-long Chinese-linked espionage operation in an isolated critical infrastructure network, a large Arch User Repository supply-chain compromise, and U.S. government...

entry.title: 152 Chrome Live Wallpaper Extensions Hid Ad Tracking and Faked Google Search Traffic
entry.content: This digest covers a browser-extension monetization cluster reported by Socket’s Threat Research Team involving 152 Chrome Web Store live wallpaper and new-tab extensions. The activity centers on deceptive data disclosures, traffic attribution manipulation, and ad-monetized redirect behavior rather than traditional credential theft, malware delivery, or page-level ad injection. The reporting is notable for the scale of the shared extension ecosystem: approximately 105,000 reported installs across 38 publisher accounts. While the extensions presented themselves as benign customization tools, Socket found inconsistencies between Chrome Web Store data-use claims and linked privacy policy language describing collection of IP addresses, ISP information, click counts, referrers, and data sharing with advertising partners. Socket identified 152 Chrome live wallpaper extensions built from a shared codebase and distributed through multiple publisher accounts. All resolved live listings reportedly claimed in the Chrome Web Store that they did not collect or use user data, while the associated privacy policies stated that telemetry such as IP address, ISP, click counts, and referrer data may be logged and shared with ad partners. A subset of 54 listings tied to tabplugins.com allegedly forged Google organic-search attribution during installation. Socket reported that these extensions used a google.com/url uninstall redirect to make extension-driven traffic appear as though it originated from Google Search, obscuring the true source of visits and potentially improving the perceived legitimacy or value of the traffic. The extensions monetized users primarily by forcing or funneling them toward ad-monetized brand pages, including tabplugins.com, yowgames.com, and chromewallpaper.com. The article emphasizes that the behavior did not involve injecting advertisements into pages users browsed; instead, the monetization model depended on controlling new-tab or extension-driven navigation flows. The campaign highlights a recurring browser-extension risk pattern: low-friction installation of consumer-facing customization utilities paired with opaque or contradictory privacy representations. In this case, the Chrome Web Store listings and linked privacy policies reportedly described user-data handling differently, creating a mismatch between public store assurances and external policy disclosures. Socket also observed that every analyzed extension shipped a service-worker routine designed to enumerate and delete IndexedDB entries. The report notes that this routine appeared inert in the analyzed build because the extensions did not store their own state in IndexedDB, but its consistent presence across the shared codebase was still part of the technical footprint connecting the listings. Overall, the reporting describes an ecosystem of Chrome extensions optimized for traffic acquisition, attribution laundering, and advertising monetization. For security teams tracking browser-extension abuse, the cluster illustrates how seemingly benign new-tab experiences can be used to shape user navigation, collect browsing-adjacent telemetry, and generate monetized traffic at scale.
entry.summary: This digest covers a browser-extension monetization cluster reported by Socket’s Threat Research Team involving 152 Chrome Web Store live wallpaper and new-tab extensions. The activity centers on deceptive data disclosures, traffic attribution manipulation, and ad-monetized redirect behavior rather...

entry.title: Shai-Hulud, CVE-2026-20253, AUR Hijacks, and Smishing Kits Drive Threat Coverage
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by software supply-chain compromise, identity and trust abuse, and high-impact vulnerabilities in widely deployed platforms. Several reports highlight attackers moving deeper into CI/CD systems, package ecosystems, and authentication infrastructure, while phishing operators continue scaling smishing operations with real-time data capture and AI-assisted tooling. Zscaler described continued evolution of the Shai-Hulud campaign, which has expanded from npm into PyPI and shifted from maintainer compromise toward CI/CD abuse. The March 2026 Miasma wave reportedly compromised Aqua Security's Trivy vulnerability scanner via GitHub Actions cache poisoning and used Python .pth files for execution at interpreter startup. The May 2026 Hades wave exploited a pullrequesttarget misconfiguration in the TanStack monorepo, scraped OIDC tokens from runner memory, and published malicious npm packages with valid SLSA provenance attestations. The campaign also introduced an AI-focused evasion technique in a June 8 Hades PyPI wave, embedding prompt injection inside an index.js bundle to influence LLM-based security scanners. ThreatLabz assesses earlier waves are linked to TeamPCP, tracked by Mandiant as UNC6780, but attribution is less certain after public release of the worm source code on May 12, 2026. A separate package ecosystem incident affected the Arch Linux AUR, where attackers hijacked more than 400 packages and modified PKGBUILD or .install scripts to run malicious npm or bun install commands during builds. Reported payloads included a Rust credential stealer distributed through atomic-lockfile@1.4.2 and a later bun-based chain using js-digest, with collection focused on browser data, developer tokens, SSH keys, shell histories, container credentials, VPN profiles, and messaging application session data. The malware also reportedly supported an optional eBPF rootkit when executed with sufficient privileges. watchTowr Labs analyzed CVE-2026-20253, a pre-authentication remote code execution issue in Splunk Enterprise 10 and later involving the PostgreSQL Sidecar Service Endpoint. The researchers reported that Splunk Enterprise on AWS ships with the sidecar installed and enabled by default, and that Splunk's web application could proxy unauthenticated requests to localhost service APIs such as /en-US/splunkd/raw/v1/postgres/recovery/backup. The reported exploit chain abused backup and restore endpoints, path traversal in the backupFile parameter, PostgreSQL connection-string behavior, and Splunk's .pgpass file to obtain controlled arbitrary file write and code execution as the splunk user. Separately, phpBB fixed a decade-old authentication bypass affecting versions 3.3.16 and below and 4.0.0-a2. Aikido reported that the flaw is exploitable in default configurations with a single HTTP request and can allow login as any user, including administrators; phpBB addressed it in version 3.3.17, while no safe 4.x release was available at publication. Censys detailed a USPS-themed smishing campaign that used a fake Cloudflare security check before serving USPS production assets from phishing infrastructure. The kit opened a WebSocket to its origin and streamed victim card data keystroke by keystroke, while performing server-side BIN lookups and returning routing decisions to the browser. Passive DNS pivots expanded one lure hostname into 682 lookalike hostnames across seven confirmed IPs as of a May 20, 2026 snapshot, with related UPS-themed infrastructure identified through banner-hash pivots. Google said it is suing a Chinese cybercrime network accused of operating the Outsider phishing-as-a-service kit and using Gemini AI to generate fraudulent phishing pages supporting SMS phishing attacks against Americans. Google attributed more than 9,000 fake websites and over 1.59 million fraudulent URLs to Outsider between November 14, 2025, and April 14, 2026, and said the service offers more than 290 templates, real-time keystroke logging, and campaign tracking dashboards. A related trust-abuse incident affected Maine's public data breach notification portal, which was temporarily disabled after fraudulent breach disclosures impersonating Discord and VRChat were submitted and automatically published. The Maine Attorney General's Office said the false reports were removed and that it had no knowledge of legitimate recent breach reports from either company. Sygnia reported that the China-nexus group Velvet Ant backdoored PAM and OpenSSH components controlling Linux logins, with earliest traces dating back to 2016. The modified authentication software allowed secret-password access, credential capture, and command logging, enabling persistence even after normal containment steps such as password resets and session termination. The targeted network reportedly lacked direct internet access, so the attackers used internet-facing systems, including a web server, as bridges into the isolated environment. The case underscores how compromise of core login components can convert authentication infrastructure itself into the persistence mechanism, complicating incident scoping and recovery.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by software supply-chain compromise, identity and trust abuse, and high-impact vulnerabilities in widely deployed platforms. Several reports highlight attackers moving deeper into CI/CD systems, package ecosystems, and authentication infrastructure, while phishing...
